modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about what you can do.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: General ability, with verbs of skill.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Adding to, third person -s.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I can drive.", "Can you swim?", "She can't cook."
examples: I can play the guitar.
Can he speak Spanish?
She can't see without glasses.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Among friends, children, casual settings.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Can you... for requests; Yes, you can. / No, you can't.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using may in casual speech (sounding too formal), can to go? word order.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Can I open the window?", "Can I use your charger?"
examples: Can I go out tonight?
Can we sit here?
You can take my pen.

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about skills in the past.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: With ability in the past (not a single event, that's managed to).
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Negative couldn't; question Could you...? for past.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using could for one specific event (I could escape -> I managed to escape).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I could run fast when I was a kid.", "She couldn't swim until she was 10."
examples: When I was five, I could already read.
He couldn't cook before he got married.
Could you speak English as a child?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Polite request, more formal than can.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: With strangers, in service.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using can when you need to be polite, adding please at beginning only.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Could you help me?", "Could you say that again?", "Could I speak to ...?"
examples: Could you close the door, please?
Could you tell me the time?
Could I have a glass of water?

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: In formal meetings, letters, with authority.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using may in casual speech, confusing with might.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "May I come in?", "May I have your attention, please?", "You may leave now."
examples: May I use your restroom?
May I ask who is calling?
Students may not use phones in class.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: When you're unsure (small chance).
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using may and might interchangeably with no nuance, though often okay.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I might go to the party.", "It might rain later.", "She might not come."
examples: He might be in his office.
I might be late tomorrow.
We might not have enough time.

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Decision made at the moment of speaking.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I'll get it! I'll help you! (not planned).
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: I think I'll...; shall I? (offer, British).
modal_and_functional_structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Immediate action verbs (get, open, answer, help).
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using going to for instant decisions.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'll answer the phone.", "I'll have the fish, please.", "I'll carry that for you."
examples: The doorbell's ringing. I'll get it.
It's cold. I'll close the window.
We're lost. I'll check the map.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I will always love you, I'll be there.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: I promise (that) I will... / I'll + verb
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: I won't tell anyone. I'll make sure.
modal_and_functional_structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of promise (call, help, never forget).
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using present continuous for promise.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'll call you tomorrow.", "I won't be late.", "I'll do my best."
examples: I'll send you the pictures.
Don't worry, I won't forget.
I'll always be there for you.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: If I were..., I would.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using would in if-clause.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'd travel if I had money.", "She wouldn't do that.", "What would you do?"
examples: I would live by the sea if I could.
He wouldn't understand.
Would you take the job?

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: To offer drinks, invitations.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: Would you like + noun/to verb?; Would you + verb + ...?
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using Do you want in formal.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Would you like some coffee?", "Would you like to dance?", "Would you join us?"
examples: Would you like a cup of tea?
Would you like to come to dinner?
Would you help me with this?

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I must remember, you must see this.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using must for past (had to), mustn't vs don't have to.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I must call my mom.", "You must try this cake.", "We mustn't be late."
examples: You must be here by 8.
I must finish this report.
You mustn't tell anyone.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: School, work, laws.
modal_and_functional_structures.essential_vocabulary_slots: Verbs of duty (wear, follow, work, attend).
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using must for rules (both possible but have to more external).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I have to wear a uniform.", "Do you have to work weekends?", "She doesn't have to come."
examples: You have to drive on the left in the UK.
He has to take medicine every day.
We don't have to stay until the end.

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Telling what's forbidden and what's not required.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Mustn't = it's not allowed; don't have to = you can if you want but not necessary.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: Mustn't + base verb; don't/doesn't have to + base verb.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Swapping meanings, e.g., You don't have to smoke here (meaning You mustn't).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You mustn't park here.", "You don't have to bring a gift.", "You mustn't use your phone."
examples: You mustn't run by the pool. / You don't have to swim if you don't want to.
We mustn't be noisy. / We don't have to whisper.
You mustn't tell her. / You don't have to tell her.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I think you should, maybe, perhaps.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Adding to, using should in past without have (should went).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You should rest.", "I think we should leave.", "Should I call him?"
examples: You should see a doctor.
He shouldn't eat so much sugar.
What should I do?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Strong advice, threat of negative consequence.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: When something bad may happen if you don't.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to, using for general advice when should is enough.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You'd better hurry.", "We'd better not be late.", "I'd better go."
examples: You'd better take an umbrella — it looks like rain.
I'd better not forget my passport.
He'd better apologize or she'll be upset.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: More common than must/have to in informal.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: don't need to = not necessary; needn't (formal); need + noun.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Need + -ing? No, need + to base.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I need to buy some milk.", "Do you need to take a break?", "She needs to talk to you."
examples: I need to go to the bathroom.
You don't need to bring anything.
He needs to get a job.

modal_and_functional_structures.structure: Be able to (ability in various tenses)
modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Express ability in forms where can is not possible (future, perfect).
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Will be able to, have been able to, used to be able to.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: be (am/is/are/was/were/will be) + able to + base verb
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using can for future (I will can), not knowing past single event (was able to manage to).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I've never been able to understand.", "Will you be able to come?", "She wasn't able to finish."
examples: I hope to be able to travel soon.
He hasn't been able to sleep.
Were you able to get tickets?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: What is expected according to rules, plans.
modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Saying what should happen (often negative: but it didn't).
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Rules, arrangements, commonly known duties.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using supposed followed by infinitive without to, forgetting d (suppose to).
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You're supposed to knock.", "It was supposed to rain.", "What are we supposed to do?"
examples: The train is supposed to arrive at 5.
You're not supposed to park here.
I was supposed to call her yesterday, but I forgot.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: In rules, when you can't use can/may for tenses.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using allowed to + -ing, missing be.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Are we allowed to take photos?", "We weren't allowed to enter.", "You're not allowed to smoke."
examples: You're allowed to use a dictionary.
He wasn't allowed to go out.
Will we be allowed to film?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Preference for one thing over another.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: I'd rather stay home; I'd rather you didn't.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: [Subject] + would rather ('d rather) + base verb (+ than) / + subject + past simple (other person preference)
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: I'd rather not; I'd rather you went.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using to base, past form wrongly when referring to other.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'd rather walk.", "I'd rather not talk about it.", "She'd rather you called later."
examples: I'd rather have tea than coffee.
He'd rather stay home tonight.
I'd rather you didn't smoke in here.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: For habitual preferences.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Prefer something to something; prefer doing to doing.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Prefer... than (should be to), using to-infinitive after prefer.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I prefer coffee to tea.", "She prefers walking.", "Do you prefer driving or flying?"
examples: I prefer summer to winter.
He prefers watching TV to reading.
Most people prefer living in the city.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: When consequences are serious.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Not + better? No, better not.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You'd better not be late.", "I'd better not forget my keys."
examples: You'd better not tell her.
We'd better not miss the bus.
He'd better not eat that.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Must be (certain true), might be (possible), can't be (certain false).
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: He must be tired, That can't be true.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using mustn't for deduction (mustn't is prohibition), confusing with can't.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You must be tired.", "That can't be right.", "He might be lost."
examples: She must be at work now.
It can't be that hard.
They might be sleeping.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Informal group planning.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Why don't we + verb; How about + verb-ing; What about + noun.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using let's with tag isn't it, wrong intonation.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Let's take a break, shall we?", "Why don't we go for a walk?", "How about eating out?"
examples: Let's watch a film, shall we?
Why don't we try the new restaurant?
How about a cup of tea?

modal_and_functional_structures.core_meaning: Polite offer (I) or suggestion (we), British English.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Offering to do something for someone, asking for decision.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: Shall I + base verb? / Shall we + base verb?
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using will I for offers.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Shall I open the window?", "Shall we dance?", "What shall we do tonight?"
examples: Shall I help you with that?
Shall we order pizza?
Shall I call a taxi?

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Asking permission in a delicate way.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: When you might disturb someone.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Do you mind if I + present; Would you mind if I + past.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Answering yes (means I do mind = no), confusing negative answer.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Would you mind opening the window?", "Do you mind if I sit here?", "Would you mind helping me?"
examples: Would you mind closing the door?
Do you mind if I use your phone?
I hope you don't mind my asking.

modal_and_functional_structures.structure: Is it okay if I...? / Can I...? variations
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: With friends, family.
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: Is it okay/ alright if I + [present simple]?
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: Can I; Could I; Do you mind if.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Overusing may in casual settings.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "Is it okay if I bring a friend?", "Can I borrow your pen?", "Is it all right if I leave early?"
examples: Is it okay if I open the window?
Can I have a glass of water?
Would it be okay if I called you later?

modal_and_functional_structures.structure: Might want to / might as well
modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Giving gentle advice, suggesting a logical course.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: "It's late, we might as well go." / "You might want to check that."
modal_and_functional_structures.pattern: [Subject] + might want to + base verb / might as well + base verb
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using should when might want to is softer.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "You might want to hurry.", "We might as well wait.", "I might as well tell you."
examples: You might want to see a doctor.
There's no bus, so we might as well walk.
I might as well finish this now.

modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: Look at those clouds.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: She's going to have a baby.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Using will instead, missing be.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "It's going to rain.", "He's going to fall!", "That chair is going to break."
examples: Look out! That cup is going to fall.
The traffic is terrible. We're going to be late.
She's going to love this present.

modal_and_functional_structures.primary_communicative_function: Talking about what is familiar, not strange anymore.
modal_and_functional_structures.when_to_use: To contrast past difficulty with present ease.
modal_and_functional_structures.key_variations: get used to (process), not used to.
modal_and_functional_structures.common_learner_mistakes: Confusing with "used to" for past habit, missing verb-ing.
modal_and_functional_structures.chunk_compatibility: "I'm used to the weather.", "She's not used to driving on the left.", "Are you used to the noise?"
examples: I'm used to waking up early.
He's not used to spicy food.
They got used to living in a small town.
